name: Zhang Fei
description: Zhang Fei, also known as Yide, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and a significant character in the historical novel *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*. He is revered as a powerful warrior, a symbol of courage, and a guardian deity, particularly in Daoist traditions. Zhang Fei was a general serving under the warlord Liu Bei during the late Eastern Han dynasty and the Three Kingdoms period. He is renowned for his impetuous nature, his booming voice, and his unmatched bravery on the battlefield. His fierce loyalty to Liu Bei and his unwavering dedication to justice cemented his place as a legendary hero. He is often depicted with a dark complexion, a bristling beard, and piercing eyes, embodying the archetype of the fierce and imposing warrior. Beyond his military prowess, Zhang Fei's story serves as a cautionary tale against unchecked anger and impulsivity, ultimately leading to his demise.
subtitle: The Fiery General

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Changban Bridge
keyMyths.description: During the Battle of Changban, Liu Bei's forces were routed by Cao Cao's army. Zhang Fei, with only a handful of soldiers, held off Cao Cao's pursuing troops at Changban Bridge. He stood alone, roaring defiantly and daring anyone to challenge him. His intimidating presence and booming voice convinced Cao Cao's army that an ambush was imminent, allowing Liu Bei to escape. This act solidified Zhang Fei's reputation as an unmatched warrior.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 42

keyMyths.title: Zhang Fei Captures Yan Yan
keyMyths.description: When Liu Bei sought to conquer Yi Province, Zhang Fei played a crucial role. He faced the veteran general Yan Yan, known for his unyielding loyalty to Liu Zhang. After a fierce battle, Zhang Fei cleverly captured Yan Yan. Impressed by Yan Yan's unwavering loyalty, Zhang Fei treated him with respect and persuaded him to join Liu Bei's forces, significantly bolstering their strength and demonstrating Zhang Fei's strategic acumen beyond brute force.
keyMyths.source: Records of the Three Kingdoms, Biography of Zhang Fei

keyMyths.title: Zhang Fei's Assassination
keyMyths.description: Despite his valor, Zhang Fei met a tragic end due to his intemperate behavior. Before a crucial campaign against Wu, he ordered his subordinates, Fan Jiang and Zhang Da, to prepare white armor within a short timeframe. When they failed to comply, Zhang Fei brutally punished them. In retaliation, Fan Jiang and Zhang Da assassinated Zhang Fei in his sleep, presenting his head to the enemy. This event highlights the consequences of unchecked anger and impulsivity.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 73

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Devotees offer incense, food, and paper money at temples dedicated to Zhang Fei, seeking his protection and blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Opera Performances
worshipPractices.description: Chinese opera frequently depicts Zhang Fei's heroic deeds, reinforcing his cultural significance and inspiring audiences.

cultural.modernLegacy: Zhang Fei remains a popular figure in modern Chinese culture, appearing in video games, films, and literature as a symbol of courage and loyalty. His image is often used in marketing to represent strength and reliability.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Oath of the Peach Garden
extendedContent.content: Details about Zhang Fei's early life are scarce, but accounts depict him as a butcher and wine seller known for his strength and fiery temper. A pivotal moment in his life was his meeting with Liu Bei and Guan Yu. The three men, drawn together by their shared ambition to restore the Han dynasty, swore an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, pledging unwavering loyalty to one another. This oath marked the beginning of their legendary journey together. Zhang Fei's impetuous nature often led to conflicts, but his loyalty to his brothers was unquestionable. He consistently demonstrated a willingness to put himself in harm's way to protect Liu Bei and Guan Yu, solidifying his place as a vital member of their burgeoning force. This bond would define their actions throughout the turbulent years to come.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Zhang Fei distinguished himself in numerous battles during the Three Kingdoms period. One notable exploit involves single-handedly holding off Cao Cao's massive army at Changban Bridge, roaring fiercely and intimidating the enemy into retreat. His bravery and intimidating presence were instrumental in securing Liu Bei's escape. He also played a crucial role in the conquest of Yi Province, demonstrating his strategic acumen alongside his martial prowess. Zhang Fei's battlefield tactics were often unconventional, relying on his overwhelming strength and fearless aggression to demoralize opponents. While sometimes prone to rash decisions, his contributions were essential to Liu Bei's military successes. His partnership with Guan Yu became legendary, and together they were a force to be reckoned with.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Deification
extendedContent.content: Zhang Fei's legacy extends far beyond his military accomplishments. He is revered as a symbol of courage, loyalty, and righteousness in Chinese culture. Following his assassination by his own subordinates due to his harsh treatment of them, Zhang Fei was posthumously granted the title 'Marquis Huan.' Over time, he transitioned from a historical figure into a mythological one, becoming a popular deity in Daoist traditions. Temples are dedicated to him, and he is often depicted as a guardian spirit, warding off evil and bringing good fortune. His image continues to inspire artists, writers, and performers, ensuring that his legend lives on through generations. Zhang Fei serves as a reminder of the importance of bravery and loyalty, but also as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked anger and impulsiveness.

shortDescription: Zhang Fei is a Chinese mythological hero renowned for his courage and martial prowess. He was a loyal general under Liu Bei during the Three Kingdoms period.
